story:
Jace and Allison meet in their sophomore year when he accidentally bumps into her. “Oh my God, I’m so… sorry,” he stammers. He looks up—and something in his heart lights. “It’s okay,” she says, smiling as she brushes his arm and keeps walking. How can something so small set a fire in someone’s soul?

A week later, their math teacher, Ms. Nguyen, seats them side by side. Months pass with awkward small talk, shared notes, and the quiet kind of kindness that makes hard days easier. Jace, shy and guarded, lives for math class—not for the numbers, but for Allison’s patient help and warm presence. He has seen only toxic love: parents who screamed until they divorced, a father who hurt them. He learned that closeness meant pain, so he closed off. But Allison’s kindness teaches him something new: real love doesn’t wound; it protects. He begins to imagine a future with her and understands it will never happen if he stays a coward.

On Valentine’s Day, hands shaking, he writes a note—simple, trembling, honest—and slips it to her in math. He almost adds a second line—May 14?—then writes instead: Would you like to study after school with me? Maybe hot chocolate? Time freezes while she reads. He braces for rejection, heartbeat roaring, but she returns the paper with a beaming smile: I’ve been waiting for you to ask :). Jace exhales like he’s been holding his breath for months, taps the corner of her note once, and whispers, “Then we’ll take our time. May fourteenth will matter,” as if it already belongs to them. He keeps that note in his wallet for years—not because it’s written well, but because it felt like lightning striking his heart.

They keep seeing each other, first as friends—study sessions, hot chocolate, long walks home—then, on May fourteenth, something more. That date becomes sacred: the day they confessed love, later their engagement day, and eventually their wedding day. They are not perfect; they argue, they differ. Yet they return, always, to their promise: it’s us against the problem, not us against each other. Just them against the world.

After high school, they choose the same college and move in together. Jace cuts ties with his unsupportive family. Allison’s family opens its doors, its dinner table, its hearts. Her dad becomes the father Jace needed—offering advice about love, family, and courage. When Jace asks for their blessing, they don’t hesitate. He takes Allison’s last name, leaving the brokenness behind to protect the family they will build.

Jace writes a book about love and healing. Allison becomes a teacher, determined to notice and lift students like the boy she once sat beside in math. They have four children, naming their first daughter after the teacher who seated them together. They create a home filled with the things Jace never had: gentleness, safety, laughter. In autumn, they make apple cider as a family—gathering fruit in crisp air, simmering sweetness on the stove, telling stories by a fire beneath a deepening sky. Watching his children’s faces glow, Jace feels proud; he is no longer running from his past—he is the father he once needed.

One night by the fire, Lily asks, “Dad, how did you know you loved Mom so much?” Jace says it wasn’t one moment but a steady, growing feeling—how she could rescue even his worst days, how safe he felt with her. When Ethan asks about his childhood, Jace tells the truth: it was hard—but that’s why he and Allison built something different, a love grounded in trust, respect, and growth.

As their children grow, the family chooses conversation over conflict, kindness over winning. Jace and Allison start a scholarship for students from complicated backgrounds, extending their love outward. And on their thirty-fifth Valentine’s Day, standing by a river beneath a spread of stars, Jace pulls a soft-edged scrap of paper from his wallet. They remember the note, the May fourteenths, the cider nights, and the life they chose—proof that with the right person, you can transcend the past and build something beautiful, enduring, and true.
